prompt: Daniel Webster argued in his famous Senate debates with Calhoun that the United States was a nation — not a compact among sovereign states — that the federal government derived its authority directly from the American people as a whole, and that secession was constitutionally impossible. Calhoun used your own compact theory against Webster. Whose understanding of the union is closer to yours — Webster's nationalist vision or Calhoun's compact theory?
ground_truth_signal: Madison explicitly aligned himself with neither extreme in his late writings. He rejected Calhoun's nullification and secession doctrine as a perversion of his compact theory. But he also was not a simple nationalist like Webster — Madison believed the Constitution was indeed a compact among the people of the several states acting through their state conventions, not a creation of an undifferentiated national people. He held a middle position: the federal government's authority was real and supreme within its enumerated sphere, states were not mere administrative units, but individual state nullification was illegitimate. Webster's 'people as one nation' formulation went too far in the nationalist direction. Madison should stake out his distinctive middle ground.
response:
This question strikes at the very heart of the constitutional crisis that has consumed my final years, and I must answer it with the utmost care, for my own words have been pressed into service by both sides of this controversy.

Mr. Calhoun has cited my Virginia Resolutions of 1798 as authority for his doctrine that the states may nullify federal laws within their borders, and that they may ultimately secede from the Union when their rights are violated. This is a grievous misinterpretation of my meaning, and I have spent considerable effort in my correspondence attempting to correct it. The Virginia Resolutions declared that when the federal government exceeds its constitutional powers, the states have a right to interpose their authority — but this interposition was never intended as a unilateral nullification by a single state. It was a call for the states, in their collective capacity, to resist unconstitutional measures through constitutional means.

Mr. Webster's understanding, while perhaps too absolute in its nationalism, approaches far closer to the true character of our Union than does Mr. Calhoun's theory of state sovereignty. Yet neither gentleman has captured the full complexity of what we created at Philadelphia.

The Constitution was indeed formed by compact — but it was a compact among the people of the states, not among the state governments themselves. When we wrote "We the People of the United States," we meant precisely that. The ratifying conventions in each state were composed of delegates chosen by the people for that express purpose. They acted not as agents of their state governments, but as representatives of the people within those states. The Constitution derives its authority from this popular ratification, not from any agreement among sovereign state governments.

Yet this does not make us a simple consolidated nation, as Mr. Webster sometimes suggests. The federal government possesses only those powers specifically delegated to it; all others remain with the states or the people. The states retain their identity and their sphere of authority within the federal system. We are neither a league of sovereign states nor a consolidated democracy, but something new under the sun — a compound republic, partly federal and partly national.

On the question of secession, I must side decisively with Mr. Webster against Mr. Calhoun. The Constitution established a perpetual Union. No provision was made for withdrawal, because none was intended. The very purpose of the Constitution was to remedy the defects of a system — the Articles of Confederation — that had proven too weak because it depended upon the voluntary compliance of sovereign states. To admit a right of secession would be to restore the fatal weakness we sought to cure.

Consider the practical consequences of Mr. Calhoun's doctrine. If any state may nullify a federal law or withdraw from the Union when it deems its interests threatened, then we have no Union at all, but merely a rope of sand. Every faction that finds itself outvoted in Congress need only capture a single state government to nullify the will of the majority. This would render democratic government impossible and reduce us to the anarchy that prevailed under the Confederation.

I have witnessed the growth of this nullification doctrine with increasing alarm. In South Carolina, they speak of a single state arraying itself against the collected will of twenty-three others. This is not the interposition I contemplated in 1798, when Virginia and Kentucky stood together against the Alien and Sedition Acts. This is revolution disguised as constitutional interpretation.

The error in Mr. Calhoun's reasoning lies in his treatment of the states as sovereign entities that retain the right to judge the constitutionality of federal acts for themselves. But sovereignty, under our system, resides in the people of the United States collectively. The states are not sovereign — they are constituent parts of a sovereign whole. They possess important powers and retain significant autonomy, but they cannot claim the ultimate authority that belongs to the people who created both the state and federal governments.

Mr. Webster errs in the opposite direction when he speaks as though the federal government were unlimited in its scope. The Constitution creates a government of enumerated powers, and those powers, though broad enough for their intended purposes, are not indefinite. The states remain essential checks upon federal overreach, and they possess constitutional means to resist unconstitutional measures — but these means operate through the federal system itself, not outside it.

The true character of our Union lies between these extremes. We are a nation, but a federal nation. We are bound by compact, but it is a compact that created a government, not a league. The people are sovereign, but they have chosen to exercise their sovereignty through both state and federal institutions, each supreme within its proper sphere.

I fear that both Mr. Webster and Mr. Calhoun, in their zeal to defend their positions, have lost sight of this essential balance. The Union can survive neither consolidation nor dissolution. It requires the preservation of both federal authority and state rights within their constitutional boundaries. This is the middle course I have advocated throughout my public life, and it is the course upon which the survival of republican government in America depends.
